data.title: ChipMOS February revenue rises sequentially
data.description:
• ChipMOS (NASDAQ:IMOS) reported February revenue of $53.4 million, up of 0.6% from the prior month, as it benefited from near-term rush orders and a further rebound in certain end markets.
  • The reported revenue, however, fell 1.2% from February 2024.

data.title: Perion Network boosts stock buyback plan by $50M
data.description:
• Perion Network (NASDAQ:PERI) has announced a $50M expansion of its share repurchase program, bringing the total authorization to $125M.
  • As of Dec. 31, 2024, the company repurchased a total of 5.2M shares, at a total amount of $46.8M.
  • Shares of Perion Network (NASDAQ:PERI) were up 1.7% premarket.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: KBR gets $85M task award by U.S. Air Force
data.description:
• KBR (NYSE:KBR) said it has been awarded a firm fixed price $85M task award by the U.S. Air Force.
  • The contract is to procure, design, and deliver critical training aids and airfield damage repair kits globally. 
  • Under the award, the firm will fabricate, design, assemble, supply, package, and deliver various types of airfield matting kits to U.S. Air Force locations worldwide.
  • The period of performance is February 2025 to February 2027, the company said.

data.title: Stock index futures trade lower as concerns about the economy grows
data.description:
Stock index futures were sharply lower on Monday as worries about the health of the economy cast a cloud of concern among investors after Donald Trump's interview with Fox News. 

S&P 500 futures (SPX) -0.9%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) -1.1%, and Dow futures (INDU) -0.8%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 4 basis points to 4.25%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 4 basis points to 3.96%.

The fears of a potential recession in the U.S. grew after Trump was asked if he was expecting a recession this year in the interview. “I hate to predict things like that,” he said. “There is a period of transition because what we’re doing is very big. We’re bringing wealth back to America. That’s a big thing.” 

"Trump also failed to rule out a recession. After the 2024 soft landing, markets are getting more concerned about economic growth risks. As the pandemic illustrated, supply chain disruption can  create growth problems. Disruption to trade and government both raise risks," UBS' Paul Donovan said.

Furthermore, adding to the bearish sentiment, China’s return to deflation has fueled worries about global growth prospects.

China's consumer prices fell 0.7% year-on-year in February, a 13-month low, exceeding the expected 0.5% drop and reversing January’s 0.5% rise.

Wall Street's major averages closed higher Friday, aided by Fed Chairman Jerome Powell's economic reassurance, but stocks sank for the week as investors wrestled with tariff twists from the Trump administration.

"Looking back, it was a truly seismic week for markets last week, as investors grappled with historic fiscal news out of Germany, European rearmament proposals, fresh tariff developments, and a U.S. jobs report on Friday," Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said.

But even as investors were enthusiastic about the fiscal news, the tariff developments led to a broader risk-off move that hit global equity markets. In fact, the S&P 500 posted its biggest weekly decline in six months, Reid said.

The economic calendar is light on Monday, with mainly the NY Fed 1-Year consumer inflation expectations on tap.

"The New York Fed’s survey of inflation expectations is due—not reliable as a prediction, but perhaps signaling whether consumers are concerned," Donovan said.

data.title: Foxconn launches its first traditional Chinese AI model FoxBrain
data.description:
Foxconn Technology (OTCPK:FXCOF) launched its first large language model called FoxBrain, with a lower-cost model training method completed in four weeks.

Foxconn — which is formally known as Hon Hai Precision (OTCPK:HNHAF) (OTCPK:HNHPF) — said the FoxBrain training process was powered by 120 of Nvidia's (NVDA) H100 GPUs, scaled with Nvidia Quantum-2 InfiniBand networking, and finished in just about four weeks.

The company added that during the model training, Nvidia provided support through its Taiwan-based supercomputer “Taipei-1” and technical consultation, enabling Hon Hai Research Institute to complete the model's pre-training with Nvidia NeMo. 

FoxBrain will also become an important engine for upgrading Foxconn’s three major platforms — Smart Manufacturing, Smart EV, and Smart City, according to the company.

The company added that FoxBrain is based on the Meta Platforms' (META) Llama 3.1 architecture with 70B parameters.

The Hon Hai Research Institute — backed by Foxconn — announced the launch of the first Traditional Chinese Large Language Model, or LLM, and noted that FoxBrain will be open sourced and shared publicly in the future.

The company, which assembles iPhones for Apple (AAPL) and also makes Nvidia's (NVDA) AI servers, said the LLM was originally designed for applications used in the Group’s internal systems, covering functions such as data analysis, decision support, document collaboration, mathematics, reasoning, problem solving, and code generation.

"Through carefully designed training methods and resource optimization, we have successfully built a local AI model with powerful reasoning capabilities," said Yung-Hui Li, Director of the Artificial Intelligence Research Center at Hon Hai Research Institute.

Foxconn said that while there was still a slight gap with DeepSeek's (DEEPSEEK) distillation model, FoxBrain performance is already very close to world-leading standards.

The company noted that the LLM shows powerful comprehension and reasoning capabilities and is optimized for Taiwanese users' language style, showing "excellent performance in mathematical and logical reasoning tests."

Foxconn added that in the future, it will continue to collaborate with technology partners to expand FoxBrain's applications, share its open-source information, and promote AI in manufacturing, supply chain management, and intelligent decision-making.

The results of FoxBrain are slated to be shared for the first time at Nvidia GTC (GPU Technology Conference) 2025 on March 20.

data.title: U.S. to cancel 83% of USAID programs, Marco Rubio says
data.description:
The U.S. is officially cancelling 83% of the programs at the U.S. Agency For International Development (USAID), said U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio on Monday.

“After a 6 week review we are officially cancelling 83% of the programs at USAID. The 5200 contracts that are now cancelled spent tens of billions of dollars in ways that did not serve, (and in some cases even harmed), the core national interests of the United States,” Rubio said in a post on X.

Rubio added that the remaining 18% of programs — around 1,000 — will be administered “more effectively” under the State Department.

Last month, Elon Musk, CEO of Tesla (TSLA) and SpaceX (SPACE), and head of the new Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) in the Trump administration, said he is taking steps to shut down USAID.

Musk said in a podcast that also featured Republicans Vivek Ramaswamy and Joni Ernst that the federal aid agency, the largest in the world, was “beyond repair” and should be dismantled.

USAID provided 42% of humanitarian aid in 2024, tracked by the U.N. As of Feb. 23, 2025, all USAID direct hire personnel, except designated personnel responsible for mission-critical functions, core leadership and/or specially designated programs, were placed on administrative leave globally.

Concurrently, the agency began implementing a reduction-in-force that will affect around 1,600 personnel with duty stations in the U.S.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

More on Elon Musk and DOGE

  • DOGE cuts potentially hitting key FDA drug testing lab – report
  • Elon Musk suggests U.S. should privatize Postal Service and Amtrak
  • Fading worries over DOGE and Elon Musk, Wedbush goes all-in on Tesla

data.title: Trump says U.S. in talks with four different groups on TikTok sale
data.description:
US President Donald Trump said that his administration is in discussions with four different groups regarding the potential sale of TikTok, owned by Chinese tech company ByteDance (BDNCE).

"We're dealing with four different groups, and a lot of people want it ... all four are good," Trump told reporters aboard Air Force One on Sunday. Talking about the deal for the social video app, he suggested it could come “soon,” but would not specify which direction he was leaning.

This development comes as TikTok faces a looming deadline to either sell its operations in the U.S. or face a ban due to national concerns. TikTok's fate in the U.S. has been uncertain since a law mandating its sale or shutdown took effect on January 19, but Trump issued an executive order delaying enforcement by 75 days to allow for negotiations. Analysts estimate TikTok could be worth up to $50B.

Trump has suggested helping broker the sale-brokering process, and he believes that the U.S. government should be given a 50% share in the business as a condition.

A potential list of buyers ranges from a group led by billionaire Frank McCourt and Reddit co-founder Alexis Ohanian, tech entrepreneur Jesse Tinsley and YouTuber MrBeast, and a merger offer from San Francisco-based Perplexity AI. Trump has mentioned Larry Ellison, the founder of Oracle (NYSE:ORCL), while TikTok has collaborated with Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) to host the data of its U.S. customers. Trump also said that Microsoft (MSFT) is among the companies keen on buying TikTok.

ByteDance (BDNCE) did not immediately respond to requests for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Coinbase stock falls as it misses out on S&P500 inclusion
data.description:
Coinbase (NASDAQ:COIN) shares fell 5% in premarket trade as the firm failed to make the cut in the latest S&P500 rebalancing.

DoorDash (DASH), TKO (TKO), Williams-Sonoma (WSM) and Expand Energy (EXE) will join the S&P 500, according to S&P Dow Jones Indices.

Interactive Brokers, Coinbase Global, Ares Management, Robinhood Markets, LPL Financial, Blue Owl Capital, and Tradeweb Markets were among the possible candidates for S&P 500 inclusion, according to a Barclays note, Barron's reported.

Other crypto-linked stocks were also down, and bitcoin (BTC-USD) fell 2.3% to $83.7K. The fall comes amid a broader risk-off sentiment among investors as President Donald Trump failed to rule out the possibility of a recession in an interview with Fox News. 

Applovin (NASDAQ:APP) was also down 6.3% as it did not make it in the latest S&P 500 rebalancing. The firm was also one of the favorites to be included in the list.

data.title: Chinese investors quietly invest millions in Elon Musk's private ventures - report
data.description:
Wealthy Chinese investors are quietly investing millions of dollars in Elon Musk's private companies including SpaceX (SPACE), xAI, and Neuralink, the Financial Times reported after speaking to China-based asset managers. 

The investments are done through special-purpose vehicles to avoid disclosing the investors' identities. Because of this, it's unclear how much Chinese capital has flowed into Musk's private firms.

Three Chinese-backed asset managers told FT that they sold Chinese investors over $30M worth of shares in SpaceX (SPACE), xAI and Neuralink over the past two years. These investments have little to do with technology transfer or influencing policy, and are primarily profit-driven, according to people involved in the deals.

The appeal of Musk's ventures has also grown in the backdrop of Beijing's restrictions on private sector companies. "China is facing an oversupply of capital and a shortage of high-quality projects," a New York investment manager told FT.

While the investments in Musk's companies are legal, they raise concerns about potential conflicts of interest as the billionaire is a senior advisor to U.S. President Donald Trump. China-based asset managers are said to be promoting Musk's ties to Trump to attract investments from rich Chinese investors.

Derek Scissors, a senior fellow at the American Enterprise Institute, claimed the influx of Chinese money into Musk's companies "adds to this picture that he is more interested in his reputation and his brand in China than he is in American interests."
